Statistical Modeling · Educational Policy

Can family engagement close the achievement gap?

A test of the compensatory hypothesis across five General Linear Models and 25,391 K-12 students, asking whether the one intervention that doesn't require money helps the students who need it most.

By Cynthia Mutua · NCES Parent & Family Involvement in Education Survey, 2016 & 2019 · R · tidymodels

Abstract

High-income students earn mostly A's at nearly twice the rate of low-income students, a gap that has held for decades. Testing whether family engagement narrows it rather than simply lifting every student equally, an income-by-engagement interaction across five models comes back negative and significant: each additional school activity lowers a low-income student's at-risk odds by 18%, against 10% for a high-income student. The effect is small in absolute terms and consistent across every model that tests it.

25,391
students analyzed, 2016 & 2019 waves
18%
stronger protective effect for low-income students
5
GLM approaches, cross-validated and compared

01 — The problem

A ZIP code still predicts a report card

High-income students earn mostly A's at 62.3%. Low-income students earn them at 36.5%. The 25.8-point gap between those numbers isn't explained by ability, it's explained by which resources a family can put behind a child's schooling, and those resources aren't distributed evenly.

Most interventions built to close that gap quietly widen it instead. Tutoring gets signed up for first by families who already have the time and the internet connection to find it. Advanced coursework goes to students whose parents know how to navigate a district's placement process. Even summer enrichment programs run into the same wall: transportation, cost, scheduling.

Family engagement looked different for one reason: it doesn't cost money. Attending a parent-teacher conference, helping with homework, showing up to a school event, none of it requires wealth. The question worth testing wasn't whether engagement helps. It clearly does. The question was whether it helps more for the students furthest behind, what education researchers call the compensatory hypothesis.

Does family engagement provide a stronger protective effect for disadvantaged students than for advantaged ones?

02 — Data & method

Building a measure that isn't noise

The dataset combines the 2016 and 2019 waves of the NCES Parent and Family Involvement in Education Survey: 25,391 K-12 students after cleaning, with grades, at-risk status, and days absent alongside eight school activities, homework involvement, cultural enrichment, income, parent education, and family structure.

A single missed PTA meeting says nothing about a family's engagement, it might just mean a schedule conflict that week. So rather than model each activity on its own, three composite measures were built to capture breadth of involvement instead of any one data point:

// composite measures, R

# school engagement, 0–8 activities school_engagement = attend_event + volunteer + general_meeting + pta_meeting + parent_teacher_conf + fundraising + committee + counselor # homework involvement, standardized homework_involvement = scale( (homework_days + homework_hours + homework_help) / 3 )[,1] # cultural enrichment, weighted composite cultural_enrichment = (story + crafts + games + projects + sports_home) + (library + bookstore) / 4 + dinners / 7

Testing the compensatory hypothesis meant testing an interaction, not just a main effect. If income and engagement are entered separately, the model can only say engagement helps on average. An income × engagement term is what lets the data say whether it helps unevenly:

// interaction term

model_spec ~ ... + income + school_engagement + income * school_engagement + ... # a negative, significant coefficient here means engagement # lowers risk more for low-income students: the compensatory signature
R 4.3tidymodelsggplot2nnetglmnetMASS

03 — Five models

One question, tested five ways

Grades, risk, and absences are three different kinds of outcomes, unordered categories, a binary flag, and a count. Each needs its own GLM, and running five lets the models check each other.

ModelResponseWhy this modelCVTest
Multinomial logistic 4 grade categories Handles unordered outcomes without assuming a rank between them. Primary model. 62.9% 62.2% Stable
Binary logistic At-risk flag Early-warning screening use case; interpretable odds ratios. 80.2% AUC 21.3% AUC Overfit
Poisson regression Days absent Count data, non-negative, no ceiling; log link fits attendance. 4.55 RMSE 4.37 RMSE Improved
Linear discriminant analysis 4 grade categories Different assumptions than logistic; a convergence check. 62.6% 62.1% Stable
Ridge regression 4 grade categories Shrinks correlated engagement measures to guard against inflated errors. 62.7% 62.0% Stable

Methodological lesson

The binary model hit 94% accuracy by predicting almost everyone as not-at-risk, useless for the students it's meant to catch, since only 6% of students are at-risk. Overall accuracy can lie when classes are this imbalanced; the fix is down-sampling, class weights, or threshold tuning before anything ships.

Robustness check

Multinomial logistic and LDA rest on different statistical assumptions, yet land within half a point of each other, 62.9% versus 62.6%. When two methods that don't agree on the math still agree on the answer, that's evidence the finding isn't an artifact of modeling choices.

Core finding

The compensatory effect holds up.

The income × engagement interaction is negative and significant, and it replicates across the multinomial, binary, and ridge models. In plain terms: the same school activity does more for a low-income student's odds than for a high-income student's.

−0.202

interaction coefficient, p = 0.009 — an effect this size would appear by chance alone less than 1% of the time.

04 — Findings

What the numbers show

High-income families still engage more, an average of 4.54 activities against 3.66 for low-income families. That 0.88-activity gap tracks more closely with structural barriers, inflexible work hours, transportation, unwelcoming school environments, than with differing interest. Where those barriers are lifted, low-income families engage at comparable rates.

What matters more than the gap in engagement is what engagement buys each group. Moving from low to high engagement, low-income students' A rate rises from 32% to 44%, a 37.5% relative increase. High-income students rise from 54% to 67%, a 24.1% relative increase. High-income students still gain more in raw percentage points, but low-income students gain more relative to where they started, which is the compensatory effect in one chart.

Not every form of engagement carries equal weight. Ranked by standardized effect on at-risk odds, homework involvement is the clear front-runner:

  1. 01

    Homework involvement

    OR = 0.59 — a 41% reduction in at-risk odds per standard deviation of involvement, the strongest single predictor in the model.

  2. 02

    Cultural enrichment

    β = -0.231, p < 0.001 — museum visits, reading, and shared activities carry a real protective effect.

  3. 03

    Parent-teacher conferences

    OR = 0.85 — low-cost, high-frequency contact that most schools already offer.

  4. 04

    School engagement

    OR = 0.90 per activity — breadth matters more than any single event attended.

05 — Policy implications

Targeting beats spreading thin

A universal engagement program, open to everyone in the same way, tends to be claimed first by families with the most slack in their schedule. The data argues for the opposite instinct: aim the outreach at the students it helps most.

Where the evidence points

  • Homework-help sessions run at school, with parents coached on supporting effort, not answers
  • Personalized outreach to low-income families, not a blanket invitation
  • Removing the actual barriers, transportation, childcare, evening scheduling
  • Parent-teacher conferences and general meetings as the low-threshold entry point
  • Dedicated support for students with disabilities, the strongest risk factor in the model

Where it falls short

  • Universal programs with no targeted outreach, captured by families with the most flexibility
  • Fundraising galas and other costly events as a primary engagement channel
  • One-size-fits-all messaging that ignores different families' actual barriers
  • Weekday daytime events that quietly exclude working parents

Why it's worth funding

~$200
per student, per year for a homework-help program
~$12,000
per student, cost of a single grade retention
18%
reduction in at-risk probability, at a fraction of the cost

06 — Discussion

What the numbers taught me

I expected engagement to lift every student by roughly the same amount. Finding instead that it does more for the students starting furthest behind was the most encouraging result in the project: it means an equitable intervention doesn't have to mean a more expensive one.

The binary model's collapse from 80% cross-validated AUC to 21% on the test set was the sharper lesson. It's tempting to report the number that looks best; the honest version of this project is the one that shows the failure alongside the fix, class weighting and threshold tuning, rather than quietly picking the model that flatters the result.

The interaction coefficient is correlational, and I'd want to strengthen the causal claim before recommending district-wide spending on it: propensity score matching on similar families who differ in engagement, natural experiments around policy changes, and longitudinal tracking to see whether the effect compounds over a student's school career.

This project began as academic coursework; the modeling choices, analysis, and everything written here are my own.

Equity doesn't always require a bigger budget. Sometimes it requires better targeting.

Five models, one consistent signal: family engagement protects low-income students more than it protects high-income ones. That's a policy lever, not just an academic result, and it's one most districts could pull tomorrow.
